title: Pressure in gases — particles in motion
examTip: Gas pressure questions almost always want a particle-level explanation — say 'more frequent collisions with the container walls' not just 'particles move faster'. And always check whether a Boyle's Law question is at constant temperature.
introTips: Pressure is caused by gas particles hitting the walls of their container — more hits or harder hits means higher pressure.
Temperature is really a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles — hotter means faster.
The Kelvin scale starts at absolute zero (0 K = −273 °C) — you must convert to Kelvin before using any gas law equation.
Boyle's Law only applies at constant temperature — if temperature changes, use the combined gas law instead.
p₁V₁ = p₂V₂ and pV/T = constant are the two equations you need to know and be able to apply.
intro: Every time you pump up a bike tyre, squeeze a balloon, or watch a weather balloon expand as it rises, you are seeing gas pressure in action. Gas pressure is not some mysterious force — it is simply the result of billions of tiny particles constantly crashing into container walls. Understanding this gives you real power: you can predict exactly what happens to pressure when you heat a gas or squash it into a smaller space. Engineers use these relationships to design everything from car engines to aerosol cans to scuba diving equipment.

keyPoints.heading: What causes gas pressure
keyPoints.content: Gas pressure is caused by gas particles colliding with the walls of their container. Each collision exerts a tiny force on the wall. Because there are enormous numbers of particles colliding every second, the total effect is a steady, measurable pressure. Pressure = force ÷ area — so more collisions per second, or harder collisions, means higher pressure. Key idea: the faster the particles move, the harder and more frequently they hit the walls → higher pressure. If you heat the gas, particles gain kinetic energy, move faster, and hit the walls harder and more often → pressure rises (if volume is fixed). If you compress the gas into a smaller volume, particles hit the walls more often per second (the same number of particles in a smaller space) → pressure rises (if temperature is fixed).
writeBullets: Gas pressure — caused by gas particles colliding with container walls
Each collision exerts a force; billions of collisions per second create measurable pressure
Higher temperature → faster particles → harder, more frequent wall collisions → higher pressure
Smaller volume → same particles in less space → more frequent wall collisions → higher pressure
Pressure = force ÷ area (Pa = N/m²)

keyTerms.def: The force per unit area exerted by gas particles colliding with container walls
A particle striking the container wall — each collision exerts a tiny force contributing to total pressure

keyPoints.memTip: More collisions OR harder collisions = higher pressure. Temperature controls speed; volume controls frequency.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Mabel needs to link cause (particle collisions) to effect (pressure) explicitly — exam mark schemes reward the phrase 'collisions with the walls'.

keyPoints.heading: Absolute zero and the Kelvin scale
keyPoints.content: Temperature in physics is measured in Kelvin (K), not just Celsius. The Kelvin scale starts at absolute zero: 0 K = −273 °C. At absolute zero, particles have the minimum possible kinetic energy — they stop moving entirely (in theory). This is the lowest possible temperature; you cannot go below it. To convert: T (K) = θ (°C) + 273. Examples: 0 °C = 273 K; 100 °C = 373 K; 20 °C = 293 K; −73 °C = 200 K. You must always use Kelvin in gas law calculations — using Celsius will give completely wrong answers because the equations require an absolute temperature scale. The Kelvin scale is important because at 0 K pressure would be zero (particles have no kinetic energy and make no collisions); this is physically meaningful in a way that 0 °C is not.
writeBullets: Absolute zero — 0 K = −273 °C — the lowest possible temperature
At absolute zero, particles have minimum kinetic energy and (theoretically) stop moving
Kelvin conversion: T (K) = θ (°C) + 273
Always use Kelvin in gas law equations — Celsius will give wrong answers

keyTerms.def: The lowest possible temperature: 0 K = −273 °C; particles have minimum kinetic energy
The absolute temperature scale; T(K) = θ(°C) + 273; must be used in gas law equations

keyPoints.memTip: Add 273 to convert °C to K. Subtract 273 to go back. 0 K = absolute zero = −273 °C.
keyPoints.tutor_note: A very common error is leaving temperature in °C in gas law calculations — always convert first.

keyPoints.heading: Boyle's Law — pressure and volume at constant temperature
keyPoints.content: At constant temperature, the pressure and volume of a fixed mass of gas are inversely proportional: p × V = constant, or: p₁V₁ = p₂V₂. If you halve the volume, the pressure doubles. If you double the volume, the pressure halves. Particle explanation: compressing the gas into half the volume means the same number of particles now bounce around in a smaller space — they hit the walls twice as often per second → pressure doubles. Units: pressure in Pa (pascals) or kPa; volume in m³ or cm³ — but both sides must use the same unit. Worked example: a gas has pressure 200 kPa and volume 3 m³. It is compressed to 1.5 m³ at constant temperature. Find the new pressure: p₂ = p₁V₁ ÷ V₂ = (200 × 3) ÷ 1.5 = 600 ÷ 1.5 = 400 kPa.
writeBullets: Boyle's Law: p₁V₁ = p₂V₂ at constant temperature
Pressure and volume are inversely proportional — one doubles, the other halves
Halve the volume → particles hit walls twice as often → pressure doubles
Units: pressure in Pa or kPa; volume in m³ or cm³ — must be consistent on both sides
Rearrange: p₂ = p₁V₁ ÷ V₂ or V₂ = p₁V₁ ÷ p₂

keyTerms.def: At constant temperature, p × V = constant for a fixed mass of gas; p₁V₁ = p₂V₂
When one quantity doubles, the other halves — their product stays constant

keyPoints.memTip: p₁V₁ = p₂V₂. Both sides equal the same constant. Just cross-multiply to rearrange.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Make sure Mabel checks that temperature is stated as constant before using Boyle's Law — if temperature changes, the combined law is needed.

keyPoints.heading: The combined gas law — pressure, volume and temperature
keyPoints.content: When temperature can also change, use the combined gas law: p₁V₁ / T₁ = p₂V₂ / T₂ (where T is in Kelvin). This combines Boyle's Law (p and V at constant T) and Gay-Lussac's Law (p and T at constant V). If volume is fixed (rigid container): p/T = constant, so doubling the Kelvin temperature doubles the pressure. Particle explanation: at higher temperature, particles move faster → hit walls harder and more often → pressure rises. Worked example: gas at 100 kPa, 2 m³, 300 K is heated to 600 K at constant volume. New pressure: p₂ = p₁ × T₂ / T₁ = 100 × 600/300 = 200 kPa. Always convert °C to K before substituting — this is the most common error in exam answers.
writeBullets: Combined gas law: p₁V₁/T₁ = p₂V₂/T₂ (T must be in Kelvin)
Fixed volume: p/T = constant — doubling Kelvin temperature doubles pressure
Higher temperature → faster particles → harder, more frequent collisions → higher pressure
Always convert temperature to Kelvin before substituting into any gas law
Rearrange for any unknown: p₂ = p₁V₁T₂ / (T₁V₂)

keyTerms.def: p₁V₁/T₁ = p₂V₂/T₂; links pressure, volume and temperature for a fixed mass of gas
At constant volume, pressure is directly proportional to Kelvin temperature: p/T = constant

keyPoints.memTip: pV/T = constant. Each fraction equals the same number. Convert to K first — always.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Encourage Mabel to write out T in Kelvin explicitly before substituting — it prevents the most common calculation error.

0.question: A sealed container of gas has a pressure of 150 kPa at a volume of 4 m³. The gas is compressed to 2 m³ at constant temperature. What is the new pressure?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! p₁V₁ = p₂V₂ → p₂ = (150 × 4) ÷ 2 = 300 kPa. Halving the volume doubles the pressure.
0.wrongFeedback: Use Boyle's Law: p₁V₁ = p₂V₂. Rearrange: p₂ = p₁V₁ ÷ V₂ = (150 × 4) ÷ 2 = 300 kPa. When volume halves, pressure doubles.

1.question: What is 27 °C converted to Kelvin?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! T(K) = θ(°C) + 273 = 27 + 273 = 300 K. Always convert to Kelvin before using gas law equations.
1.wrongFeedback: Convert using: T(K) = θ(°C) + 273 = 27 + 273 = 300 K. You must add 273, not use the Celsius value directly.

3.question: A gas in a fixed volume container is at 200 kPa and 300 K. It is heated to 450 K. What is the new pressure?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! At fixed volume, p/T = constant: p₂ = p₁ × T₂/T₁ = 200 × 450/300 = 300 kPa.
3.wrongFeedback: At fixed volume, p₁/T₁ = p₂/T₂. So p₂ = p₁ × T₂/T₁ = 200 × (450/300) = 300 kPa. Temperature must be in Kelvin.

commonMistakes: Using Celsius instead of Kelvin in gas law equations — always convert: T(K) = θ(°C) + 273 before substituting.
Applying Boyle's Law when temperature also changes — Boyle's Law only works at constant temperature; use pV/T = constant if T changes.
Giving a vague explanation of pressure increase ('particles move faster') without saying 'more frequent/harder collisions with the walls' — the walls phrase is needed for full marks.
Mixing up units within an equation — pressure must be in the same units (both Pa or both kPa) on each side; same for volume.
Saying pressure increases when volume increases — it is the opposite: bigger volume → particles hit walls less often → pressure falls.
examTips: Always convert temperature to Kelvin (add 273) before substituting into any gas law — this is the most penalised error.
Particle explanations must mention collisions with the container walls, not just particle speed — you need both parts for full marks.
For Boyle's Law calculations, check: does the pressure go up when volume goes down? If yes, your answer is physically reasonable.
State which law you are using and write out the equation before substituting — this gains method marks even if you make an arithmetic error.
revisionCardBullets: Gas pressure caused by particle collisions with container walls
Higher temperature → faster particles → more frequent, harder collisions → higher pressure
Smaller volume → more frequent collisions per unit area → higher pressure
Absolute zero: 0 K = −273 °C — lowest possible temperature
Volume halves → pressure doubles (at constant temperature)
Combined gas law: p₁V₁/T₁ = p₂V₂/T₂ (T in Kelvin)
Fixed volume: p/T = constant — doubling Kelvin temperature doubles pressure
Pressure units: Pa (pascals); volume units: m³ or cm³ — must be consistent
